lens: Novelty of the positioning: has anyone already published a cross-prover or cross-scheme PQ signature benchmark inside general-purpose zkVMs?
reasoning:
Six fresh queries plus four fetches. I could not refute the narrow claim, but I found one source that materially weakens two of its qualifiers, and I re-verified that the closest competitor is still frozen.

=== NEW AND MATERIAL: IACR ePrint 2026/525 / AsiaCCS ===
"SoK: Understanding zkVM: From Research to Practice", Yunbo Yang, Yuejia Cheng, Haibo Tang, Guomin Yang, Bingsheng Zhang, Kui Ren. ePrint landing page (https://eprint.iacr.org/2026/525) FETCHED SUCCESSFULLY, gives verbatim "Received 2026-03-16; Last revision 2026-03-24". Also published by ACM, DOI 10.1145/3779208.3805964, listed in search as "Proceedings of the ACM Asia Conference on Computer and Communications Security", i.e. AsiaCCS. Abstract quoted verbatim ends: "we conduct a comprehensive experimental evaluation of representative zkVMs following this layered framework."

Why it matters: per search-surfaced content from the PDF and from the Fenbushi article covering the same matrix, this line of work evaluates EIGHT zkVMs, named verbatim in search output as "SP1, RISC Zero, OpenVM, Pico, ZKM, Jolt, Nexus, and Novanet", across "four computational tasks and three performance metrics (prover time, proof size, and peak RAM utilization)". The four workloads are given verbatim as "memory consumption for the Fibonacci, SHA2, ECDSA, and ETHTransfer benchmarks", with "Fibonacci: Calculation of the 100,000th Fibonacci number", "SHA2: SHA2-2048 hash computation", "ECDSA: ECDSA signature verification using the secp256k1 curve".

So: a peer-reviewed, 8-prover zkVM benchmark already exists using EXACTLY our three headline metrics (prover time, proof size, peak RAM), already including both SP1 and RISC Zero, and already including a secp256k1 ECDSA signature-verification workload as one of its four tasks.

CRUCIALLY: it contains NO post-quantum signature workload. No ML-DSA, no Falcon, no SLH-DSA, no Dilithium, no SPHINCS+ appears in any of its four named tasks. So it does NOT occupy the cell and does NOT refute the claim as worded. What it does is kill any implied novelty in the METHOD: "cross-prover", "prover time + peak memory + proof size", and "classical ECDSA control measured in the same harness" are all established, published, peer-reviewed practice. Only the SCHEME axis (the three FIPS schemes) is actually empty.

=== LOUD METHODOLOGICAL DISCONFIRMATION ON THAT SOURCE ===
I could NOT read the SoK PDF directly. https://eprint.iacr.org/2026/525.pdf returned HTTP 403 Forbidden (consistent with the known Cloudflare block on eprint PDFs recorded in the corpus for 2021/1048). https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3779208.3805964 also returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. https://web.archive.org/web/2026/... was refused outright by the fetcher ("Claude Code is unable to fetch from web.archive.org"), so the Wayback fallback that the corpus recommends for eprint is NOT available to me in this environment. The zkVM list, the workload list, and the three metrics are therefore taken from SEARCH-ENGINE SNIPPETS of the PDF and of the Fenbushi write-up, NOT from a document I opened. They are quoted accurately as returned, but they are NOT directly verified. Nobody should put the SoK's workload list into LITERATURE.md until a human opens that PDF in a browser. If the SoK turns out to include a PQ workload after all, this verdict flips to refuted.

=== RE-VERIFIED, NO CHANGE ===
s2morrow (starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow): I re-fetched https://github.com/starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow/commits/master/ and confirmed the most recent commit is still "fix blake2s hasher (#7)" by m-kus, dated March 23, 2026, with nothing after it. Four months cold. Its "Stwo proving benchmarks" deliverable remains unshipped. The corpus finding stands unchanged. (Note: https://api.github.com/repos/starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow/commits returned HTTP 403 to the fetcher, so I used the HTML commits page instead.)

=== LAST-60-DAYS CHECK ===
https://pq.ethereum.org/ fetched successfully, visible date verbatim "Last updated: June 27, 2026", i.e. inside the 60-day window. It names leanXMSS, leanVM, Falcon, Dilithium and SPHINCS+, but publishes NO prover time, NO memory, NO proof size. Qualitative only. Not a threat.
leanroadmap.org and the leanEthereum material continue to describe the zkVM signature-aggregation track (Binius M3, SP1, KRU, STU, Jolt, OpenVM) as exploratory with no published cross-prover PQ numbers, consistent with the corpus.
Searches for ML-DSA/Dilithium verification inside RISC Zero or SP1 with published cycle counts returned only native/embedded PQC benchmarks (Cortex-M0+/RP2040, Zephyr/QEMU, general CPU testbeds) and the already-known Lattice and ZK-ACE preprints. No new zkVM-side PQ measurement surfaced.

=== BOTTOM LINE ===
Nothing found publishes measured prover cost for ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512 or SLH-DSA-128s inside RISC Zero or SP1. The claim survives literally. But it now has a strong, peer-reviewed, same-metrics, same-provers neighbour that a reviewer will find in one search, and the claim's phrasing invites the reader to credit us for the cross-prover harness and the classical control, which are not ours to claim.
counterevidence:
1. IACR ePrint 2026/525, "SoK: Understanding zkVM: From Research to Practice" (Yang, Cheng, Tang, Yang, Zhang, Ren), https://eprint.iacr.org/2026/525, landing page fetched 2026-07-23, verbatim "Received 2026-03-16; Last revision 2026-03-24"; ACM DOI 10.1145/3779208.3805964 (AsiaCCS proceedings). Search-surfaced content (PDF 403, ACM 403, NOT directly read): eight zkVMs "SP1, RISC Zero, OpenVM, Pico, ZKM, Jolt, Nexus, and Novanet"; "four computational tasks and three performance metrics (prover time, proof size, and peak RAM utilization)"; workloads "Fibonacci, SHA2, ECDSA, and ETHTransfer", with "ECDSA: ECDSA signature verification using the secp256k1 curve". Contains no PQ signature workload.
2. Fenbushi Capital, "Benchmarking zkVMs: Current State and Prospects", https://fenbushi.vc/2025/08/29/benchmarking-zkvms-current-state-and-prospects/ (dated 2025-08-29) and the Medium mirror. Same 8-prover matrix, same three metrics. Verbatim figures returned in search: RISC Zero 100,000th Fibonacci "3.6 seconds", GPU memory "0.63 GB", proof size "222 kB"; OpenVM SHA2 "0.99 seconds"; Ethereum transaction simulation "7.6 seconds"; proof sizes "SP1 produces proofs of about 1.8 MB, RISC0 produces proofs of about 209 KB, and Jolt remains between 66 and 75 KB". No PQ schemes.
3. https://pq.ethereum.org/, visible date verbatim "Last updated: June 27, 2026". Names leanXMSS, leanVM, Falcon, Dilithium, SPHINCS+. Publishes zero benchmark numbers.
4. https://github.com/starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow/commits/master/ re-fetched 2026-07-23: newest commit still "fix blake2s hasher (#7)", March 23, 2026. No new work, benchmarks still unpublished.
5. FETCH FAILURES TO DISCLOSE: eprint.iacr.org/2026/525.pdf HTTP 403; dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3779208.3805964 HTTP 403; api.github.com HTTP 403; web.archive.org blocked entirely by the fetcher, so the corpus's standard Wayback fallback for eprint PDFs is unavailable here.
recommended_change:
Keep the claim, but stop letting "cross-prover", "same-rig classical control" and the three-metric set read as our contribution, because AsiaCCS 2026 already published all three across eight zkVMs including SP1 and RISC Zero with a secp256k1 ECDSA workload.

Rewrite the positioning to put the entire weight on the scheme axis:

"Cross-prover zkVM benchmarking is established practice: the AsiaCCS 2026 SoK measures prover time, proof size and peak RAM for eight zkVMs (SP1 and RISC Zero among them) on Fibonacci, SHA2, secp256k1 ECDSA and ETHTransfer. Every published matrix of this kind stops at classical signatures. We extend that same methodology to the three FIPS post-quantum signature schemes, ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512 and SLH-DSA-128s, with standards-conformant hashing (SHAKE, not Poseidon or RPO substitutes), an Ed25519/ECDSA control in the same harness, and a batch-size sweep. We are not claiming the harness; we are claiming the empty column in it."

Three concrete edits:
1. Delete or heavily qualify any sentence implying we are first to benchmark across provers or first to include a classical control. Cite ePrint 2026/525 in the same paragraph as the claim, not buried in LITERATURE.md.
2. Add ePrint 2026/525 to LITERATURE.md as the METHODOLOGICAL baseline (alongside leanBench and ZK-ACE Table 7), explicitly noting it is peer-reviewed, covers eight provers, and contains no PQ workload. That last clause is what protects our novelty, so it must be stated in our own copy before a reviewer states it against us.
3. BLOCKER before publishing: a human must open https://eprint.iacr.org/2026/525.pdf in a browser and confirm the four workloads and the eight-prover list. The PDF, the ACM page and Wayback all refused automated fetches, so our current workload list is search-snippet-sourced and unverified. If that PDF turns out to contain any PQ signature workload, this claim is refuted and the positioning must be rebuilt.

lens: Build-plan feasibility on Apple M3 Max / 64 GB / no CUDA / ~$0 budget
reasoning:
I tried to kill this on the build plan and could not kill the CLAIM, but the PLAN as scoped is not deliverable. Findings in order of what actually matters.

1) THE ATTACK I EXPECTED TO WIN, AND LOST. "The PQ crates will not compile to guests" is FALSE. All three FIPS schemes have mature pure-Rust no_std crates, verified by me on crates.io today (2026-07-23): ml-dsa v0.1.1 (2026-06-05, RustCrypto, "Pure Rust implementation of ML-DSA ... as described in FIPS-204 (final)", 1,429,402 downloads); fips204 v0.4.6 (2024-12-22, category "No standard library", 569,937 downloads) as a second source; slh-dsa v0.2.0-rc.5 (2026-04-28, RustCrypto, FIPS-205); fn-dsa v0.4.0 and fn-dsa-vrfy v0.4.0 (both updated 2026-07-22, Pornin, category "No standard library"). fn-dsa-vrfy being a separate verify-only crate matters: Falcon's floating-point exposure is in signing, and verification is integer-only, so the RV32IM/RV64IM soft-float trap does not bite the workload you are actually proving. Concede this point publicly; do not put "will the crates build" in the risk register.

2) THE OOM ATTACK ALSO LOSES, FOR A REASON THE PLAN SHOULD STATE. Batch=1000 will not OOM, because in both provers peak prover RAM is set by the SEGMENT/SHARD size, not the session size. RISC Zero documents this explicitly on ExecutorEnvBuilder::segment_limit_po2: "Lowering this value will reduce the memory consumption of the prover. Memory consumption is roughly linear with the segment size, so lowering this value by 1 will cut memory consumpton by about half" [sic, docs' own typo], bounded to [MIN_CYCLES_PO2 = 13, MAX_CYCLES_PO2 = 24]. Input data is trivial: 1000 x (1312 B pk + 2420 B sig) = 3.73 MB against ~3 GB of guest user memory. SP1 docs ask only "16GB+" for Core/Compress. 64 GB is not the binding constraint.

3) WHAT ACTUALLY KILLS IT: WALL CLOCK, AND IT IS NOT CLOSE. Because prover time is LINEAR in N (your own established finding, independently corroborated by leanBench's committed JSON where flat_125 -> flat_1000 goes 1,331,475,651 ns -> 9,922,054,126 ns, i.e. 7.45x for 8x the signatures), the N=1000 point costs ~1000x the N=1 point. The only measured RISC Zero anchor for a PQ signature workload in the corpus is HAPPIER's raw logs (r0vm 1.2.5), where a single leaf proof takes 490.666834125 to 618.510015833 seconds, on a machine the abstract calls "a standard laptop" and never specifies. That is 8 to 10 minutes for ONE proof of a far simpler hash-based workload with no lattice arithmetic. Nobody publishes cycles/sec for r0vm on Apple Silicon; I searched and could not find it, and babybear-labs/benchmark's raw README 404s. So the project cannot even budget its own compute from published data, which is itself a finding.
Do the multiplication the plan has not done. Deliverable as worded = 3 PQ schemes + Ed25519 + ECDSA = 5 schemes, x 2 provers = 10 lanes, x a batch sweep, x >=3 repeats for a median. A geometric sweep's total is ~2x its largest term, so the N=1000 point dominates everything. If one ML-DSA-44 in-guest verify proves in 10 s (optimistic: Metal, RISC Zero, no NTT precompile in either prover), N=1000 is ~2.8 h for one run of one scheme on one prover; 3 repeats x 5 schemes ~= 42 h on RISC Zero alone, and SP1 on this box is scalar CPU with no CUDA and no AVX (AVX is Intel-only, CUDA is "only supported on Linux x86_64"), so call it 3-5x more. The N=1000 column alone lands at roughly 1 to 2 weeks of continuous compute; the full sweep is ~3 weeks of 24/7 laptop time. On a thermally throttled laptop, with a non-coder owner, with zero slack, where any harness change invalidates every number already collected.

4) THE WORST CELL IS SLH-DSA-128s AND THE PLAN DOES NOT KNOW IT. Native verification cycles from nist-sigs-zoo data/schemes: ML-DSA-44 verification_cycles 76,028; Falcon-512 65,140; SLH-DSA SHAKE-128s 2,682,410. SLH-DSA-128s verification is ~35x ML-DSA-44 natively, and it is nearly pure SHAKE with no NTT to accelerate. In-guest, unaccelerated, that is the single most expensive cell in the entire matrix, and at N=1000 it plausibly runs to tens of billions of cycles and produces thousands of segments whose continuation recursion is itself a cost multiplier absent from the naive estimate. If any cell is simply unreachable on this hardware, it is SLH-DSA-128s at large N.

5) A CLAIM-INTEGRITY PROBLEM CREATED BY THE HARDWARE, NOT BY TIME. "RISC Zero and SP1 ... measured on the same rig" is, on an M3 Max, a comparison of Metal GPU proving against scalar CPU proving. RISC Zero docs: on Apple silicon "RISC Zero will use the integrated Metal compute cores." SP1 docs: "GPU proving is only supported on Linux x86_64", and AVX is Intel-only. Worse, the ISAs differ: SP1 v6 guests target riscv64im-succinct-zkvm-elf (corroborated by SP1's own documented error about a missing riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc) while RISC Zero targets riscv32im. So neither the seconds nor the cycles are comparable across the two provers. The claim does not promise a RANKING, so it survives, but any chart that puts the two on one axis is indefensible and a reviewer will say so in one sentence.

6) GROTH16 IS A NON-ISSUE BECAUSE IT IS OUT OF SCOPE. RISC Zero docs are unambiguous: "The Groth16 prover currently _only_ works on x86 architecture, and so Apple Silicon is _currently unsupported_ (even via Docker)." SP1 documents Docker plus ~14 GB for the Groth16 wrap and 64 GB+ for PLONK. But the claim under attack asks for wall-clock, peak memory, proof size and code. It never mentions on-chain proofs. Do not build the Groth16 leg; it cannot be done symmetrically here and it buys nothing the claim promises.

7) ONE MORE HAZARD THE PLAN SHOULD PIN. fn-dsa and fn-dsa-vrfy both moved to v0.4.0 on 2026-07-22, one day before this assessment. Benchmarking against a crate released yesterday, with 18 downloads on the new version, is a reproducibility hazard. Pin exact versions and record them in the results JSON.

NET: the positioning claim is not refuted by feasibility. Every qualifier in it is achievable on this hardware at reduced batch sizes. What is refuted is the implicit build plan behind the words "swept across batch size" if that sweep is meant to reach ~1000.
counterevidence:
Evidence that cuts AGAINST my own attack, stated plainly so it is not hidden:
- The crate-availability attack fully fails. Verified no_std pure-Rust implementations exist for all three FIPS schemes, including a verify-only Falcon crate that sidesteps the floating-point concern entirely (fn-dsa-vrfy v0.4.0, crates.io, accessed 2026-07-23).
- The OOM attack fails. Segment/shard-bounded prover memory means batch size does not drive peak RAM; RISC Zero documents the knob (segment_limit_po2) and its roughly-linear memory relationship directly.
- RISC Zero on this exact machine is the BEST-case consumer platform: Metal acceleration is automatic and documented, which is better than the corpus's own SP1 situation and better than a generic laptop.
- Cycle counts are FREE. RISC Zero's Executor::execute() "only executes the program and does not generate a receipt" and returns SessionInfo with cycles(); SP1's client.execute(...).run() returns an ExecutionReport with total_instruction_count(), plus syscall_counts and per-region cycle_tracker under the profiling feature. So the entire scheme x batch matrix of CYCLE COUNTS is obtainable in hours, not weeks, on this hardware, for both provers. That is a genuinely novel deliverable (nobody has published ML-DSA-44 zkVM cycle counts) and it is fully feasible at $0.
- ZK-ACE published N = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 only, and that was sufficient for a peer-visible arXiv result demonstrating the flat-amortization shape. Precedent supports a small sweep.
- My time estimates rest on an assumed 10 s/signature proving figure that is NOT PUBLISHED by anyone for Apple Silicon. I am explicitly labelling that as my inference from the HAPPIER logs (490 to 618 s per leaf proof, r0vm 1.2.5, hardware unnamed) and from leanBench's x86 numbers. If ML-DSA-44 proves in 1 s/signature on Metal, my three-week figure collapses to a couple of days and the plan is fine as written. The honest move is to MEASURE N=1 first and derive the budget, not to trust my number or the plan's silence.
recommended_change:
Six changes, in priority order. #1 is the one that saves the project.

1. KILL BATCH=1000. Sweep N in {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32} and stop. Your own established finding is that amortized prover cost per signature is FLAT in N and only proof bytes amortize, and leanBench + ZK-ACE both independently reproduce that shape. Large-N points therefore add ZERO information while consuming ~30x the compute. ZK-ACE published N=1..16 and it was enough. This single change turns a three-week 24/7 laptop run into a day or two.

2. MEASURE-THEN-BUDGET GATE. Before committing to any sweep, run exactly one cell: ML-DSA-44, N=1, RISC Zero, prove it, record wall clock. Multiply by your intended matrix and by N. If the projected total exceeds ~48 h of machine time, cut scope again before writing any more harness code. Do not let a non-coder owner discover the budget problem in week two.

3. SHIP CYCLE COUNTS FIRST, AS THEIR OWN RELEASE. Execute-only (RISC Zero SessionInfo::cycles() plus per-segment SegmentInfo; SP1 ExecutionReport::total_instruction_count() plus syscall_counts and cycle_tracker under the profiling feature) costs hours, needs no GPU, cannot OOM, and fills the empty cell on its own. Publish the full 5-scheme x 6-batch x 2-prover cycle matrix, then prove only a subset. Report both raw cycles() and po2-padded per-segment totals, since cycles() explicitly excludes continuation overhead and padding and would otherwise understate real prover work.

4. CUT TO ONE PROVER FOR v0.1. Ship RISC Zero only (it has Metal on this box), ML-DSA-44 plus an Ed25519 control, N in {1,2,4,8,16}, on the named M3 Max. That is a real, novel, defensible result reachable in days. Add SP1, then Falcon-512, then SLH-DSA-128s as v0.2/v0.3. Keep the headline claim's wording for the eventual full release, but do not promise the full matrix in the first publication.

5. FIX THE CROSS-PROVER CHART BEFORE IT EXISTS. Never put Metal-accelerated RISC Zero and scalar-CPU SP1 seconds on the same axis, and never compare their cycle counts at all (riscv32im vs riscv64im are different ISAs). Either (a) force RISC Zero to CPU-only via its documented CPU/GPU feature flags for the head-to-head run and publish the Metal number separately as best-case, or (b) present two independent single-prover columns with an explicit "these are not comparable to each other, and here is why" note. Also keep the round-1 disclosure in every chart: neither prover has a lattice/NTT precompile while both accelerate Ed25519/ECDSA, so the PQ penalty shown is partly a precompile-availability artifact.

6. DROP GROTH16 ENTIRELY, and say why in the README. RISC Zero cannot do it on Apple Silicon "even via Docker" per its own docs, SP1 needs Docker plus ~14 GB, and the claim does not ask for on-chain proofs. Removing it also removes the only place where 64 GB might have been tight.

Two smaller items: pin exact crate versions in the results JSON (fn-dsa/fn-dsa-vrfy v0.4.0 shipped 2026-07-22, one day before this assessment, and is a moving target), and schedule SLH-DSA-128s LAST at small N only, prepared to publish "N=1 only; larger N exceeded our compute budget on this hardware" as an honest and still-publishable result, since its native verify is ~35x ML-DSA-44's and it is nearly pure SHAKE.

lens: Hostile cryptographer reviewer attacking methodology: cross-prover comparability, soundness/security-parameter mismatch, cycle accounting, laptop-benchmark defensibility, and whether "amortized dollar cost per transaction" is a real metric.
reasoning:
The claim's factual core survives: the FIPS-in-general-purpose-zkVM cell is genuinely empty, and nothing in the sentence is false. I could not refute it. But as a reviewer I would not accept the COMPARISONS the wording implies, and three sub-claims are fatal as written. Everything below is from sources I fetched today (2026-07-23).

O1. FATAL AS WORDED: "RISC Zero and SP1" on one axis is not a comparison. I pulled RISC Zero's actual constants from risc0/zkp/src/lib.rs: `pub const QUERIES: usize = 50;` with the verbatim source comment "50 FRI queries are sufficient to achieve our security target of 97 bits (conjectured security)", and `pub const INV_RATE: usize = 4;` (log_blowup = 2). Meanwhile dev.risczero.com/api/security-model states the RISC-V Prover is "96" bits and relies on the "Toy Problem Conjecture". So RISC Zero's own two artifacts disagree with each other (97 in source, 96 in docs), and both say conjectured. SP1 v6 is Hypercube, and docs.succinct.xyz/docs/sp1/security/security-model says verbatim: "SP1 Hypercube does not rely on proximity gap conjectures. Rather, it relies on proximity gap theorems established in ... while operating in the unique decoding regime", targeting 100 bits. The killer detail: RISC Zero's 50 queries at blowup 4 is EXACTLY SP1 Turbo's own published rule (`num_queries = 100 / log_blowup`, so 100/2 = 50). Identical parameterization, labeled 96-97 bits by one vendor and 100 by the other. The bit-labels are vendor accounting conventions, not commensurable units. Worse, v6 Hypercube is a different ASSUMPTION CLASS (proven, unique decoding regime) versus RISC Zero (conjectured), and the unique decoding regime costs more queries per security bit. If SP1 measures slower, part of that is soundness, not the PQ scheme. A bar chart at default settings is uninterpretable.

O2. FATAL AS WORDED: the Ed25519/ECDSA "control" is not a control. Two variables change at once: the signature scheme AND precompile availability. Both zkVMs accelerate Ed25519/ECDSA; neither has an NTT or lattice precompile. So the measured ratio is (PQ penalty) x (precompile asymmetry) and the paper cannot separate them. Compounding this: RISC Zero DOES ship a patched `tiny-keccak` (Keccak-f[1600], stabilized in v3.0.1, gated behind `unstable`), so SHAKE can be accelerated there. That means there is no single "the" ML-DSA-44 cost on RISC Zero; the number depends entirely on whether you patch. Publishing one figure without the stock-versus-patched pair is a choice a reviewer will read as cherry-picking in whichever direction it flatters.

O3. FATAL AS WORDED: "on the same rig" does not mean same conditions. On the M3 Max, RISC Zero automatically uses Metal; SP1 has no GPU path on macOS aarch64 (CUDA is Linux x86_64 only, AVX is Intel-only). The headline cross-prover number is GPU-versus-scalar-CPU. "Same rig" is technically true and materially misleading, which is the worst category of methods claim.

O4. Cycles are three different units. SP1 v6 guests target riscv64im (RV64IM); RISC Zero targets riscv32im (RV32IM). Different word size means a 64-bit multiply is one instruction versus several. SP1 charges a flat +256 clock per syscall regardless of which precompile. RISC Zero pads segments to powers of two and `SessionInfo::cycles()` explicitly excludes continuation overhead and po2 padding, so it understates proven work. Any table with a shared "cycles" column is wrong.

O5. The laptop is defensible only with a protocol, and without one the bias runs in the worst possible direction. M3 Max does throttle under sustained all-core load, and the 14-inch throttles more than the 16-inch; reviewers cite High Power Mode and ambient temperature as material. A batch sweep run in monotone order (N = 1, 2, 4, ... 1024) puts the largest N last and hottest, confounding batch size with heat soak in exactly the axis of the headline chart. Separately, RISC Zero's docs warn Metal kernels JIT-compile on first run and "can take a few minutes"; if run #1 is not discarded, that lands entirely in the smallest-N bucket. Those two artifacts together can manufacture a fake amortization curve out of pure thermodynamics.

O6. "Peak memory" is currently a tunable, not a measurement. RISC Zero peak prover RAM is governed by `segment_limit_po2`, whose docs state "lowering this value by 1 will cut memory consumption by about half", and whose default is not published. Unrecorded means irreproducible.

O7. "Proof size" is not one number and not comparable. RISC Zero's Groth16 prover "only works on x86 architecture, and so Apple Silicon is currently unsupported (even via Docker)". SP1's Groth16 needs Docker and roughly 14 GB for the wrap. So the RISC Zero column would be a succinct receipt and the SP1 column a 260-byte Groth16 proof. That is a category error, and 260 bytes is a fixed SP1 property, not a result of this work.

O8. "Amortized dollar cost per transaction" is, as currently sourceable, a marketing number. Bonsai shut down December 2025 and Boundless is a bid-based market with no published rate, so any $/tx is laptop-seconds multiplied by a cloud rate for hardware you did not use. It is a modeling assumption wearing a measurement's clothes.

O9. The batch sweep is answered prior art, not a finding. ePrint 2021/1048 Table 3, the Winterfell README, ZK-ACE Table 7 (prove/tx flat at 14.29 to 15.76 ms while proof/tx falls 122 to 13 KB), and leanBench's committed JSON all show it. Leading with it invites a one-sentence dismissal.

O10. Feasibility ceiling. RISC Zero guest user memory is roughly 3 GB total for code, heap and stack, and prover RAM grows linearly. ePrint 2021/1048 needed 7.4 GB for 1024 Lamport+ signatures, and a FIPS scheme in a general zkVM is far heavier. On 64 GB you will cap out at small N, and a sweep reaching only N = 8 or 16 does not support a curve.

O11. "Standards-conformant" is an assertion until there is a conformance artifact. Falcon is FIPS 206 DRAFT, so "FIPS-conformant Falcon" unqualified is wrong. And the field already contains a fake: OpenZeppelin's ML-DSA-44 crate is a stub whose `verify` returns true for any 43-felt key and 79-felt signature. Without in-guest known-answer tests plus a negative test, a reviewer cannot distinguish your verifier from that one.

VERDICT: I would cite the measurement and refuse the comparison. The work is publishable and useful; the framing is not defensible as stated. All fixes are cheap, which is why this is major rather than fatal.
recommended_change:
1. REWRITE THE CLAIM to drop implied cross-prover comparability and the word "control". Proposed: "We publish measured prover cost (wall-clock, peak RSS, proof size, cycles, named hardware, runnable code) for ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512 and SLH-DSA-128s verified inside two general-purpose zkVMs, RISC Zero v3.0.6 and SP1 v6.3.1, each at its own disclosed security parameters, alongside Ed25519/ECDSA measured in the same harness as a PRECOMPILE-ADVANTAGED REFERENCE (not a control), across batch sizes up to the largest N each prover completes on the rig. To our knowledge these are the first published prover-cost figures for the three FIPS schemes in a general-purpose zkVM; amortization behaviour replicates the known flat-per-signature result rather than discovering it."

2. PUBLISH A SECURITY-PARAMETER ROW ON EVERY CHART, and never a bare cross-prover bar. Record per run: target bits as claimed by the vendor, queries, blowup/inv_rate, PoW/grinding bits, and assumption class (conjectured vs proven). State explicitly that RISC Zero (QUERIES=50, INV_RATE=4, "97 bits (conjectured security)" in source versus "96" in docs, Toy Problem Conjecture) and SP1 v6 Hypercube (100 bits, proximity gap THEOREMS, unique decoding regime) are not the same security level or the same assumption class, and that the unique decoding regime costs more per security bit. Add a one-line caveat under every cross-prover figure: "not a like-for-like comparison."

3. MAKE CYCLES THREE LABELED COLUMNS, never one. Report RISC Zero raw `SessionInfo::cycles()` AND po2-padded per-segment totals AND `segment_limit_po2` actually used; report SP1 `total_instruction_count()` AND `total_syscall_count()` AND prover gas. Label the ISA (RV32IM vs RV64IM) in the column header so nobody sums them.

4. RUN THE PRECOMPILE MATRIX, both ways, for every scheme: stock `sha3` vs RISC Zero patched `tiny-keccak`; SP1 stock vs sha3/SHAKE precompile; Ed25519 with and without its precompile. The delta is itself a publishable result and it is the only way the PQ-vs-classical ratio becomes interpretable.

5. FIX THE HEAD-TO-HEAD BACKEND. Either force RISC Zero to CPU-only via its documented feature flags for the apples-to-apples run and publish the Metal number separately as best-case, or drop the cross-prover chart entirely. Do not ship GPU-vs-scalar-CPU under the phrase "same rig".

6. THERMAL PROTOCOL, pre-registered and published: AC power, High Power Mode state, chassis (14 vs 16 inch), ambient temperature, INTERLEAVED/randomized run order (never monotone in N), n >= 10 with median and IQR plus raw samples retained (copy leanBench's `samples_ns`), discard run #1 for Metal kernel JIT, and a drift check that re-runs the first configuration last and publishes the delta. Without the interleave, the batch curve is confounded with heat soak.

7. SPLIT PROOF SIZE by receipt type and do not cross-compare. RISC Zero on Apple Silicon: composite/succinct only, and say plainly that Groth16 is x86-only "even via Docker". If an on-chain number is needed, run that leg on x86-64 Linux and label it as a different rig. Never present SP1's 260 bytes as a result of this work.

8. DROP $/tx FROM THE HEADLINE. If shown at all, put it in an appendix as an explicitly derived quantity with instance type, hourly rate, quote date, and utilization assumption, plus a note that Bonsai shut down in December 2025 and Boundless is a bid market with no published rate. Make prover-seconds and peak RSS on named hardware the primary metrics.

9. SHIP A CONFORMANCE ARTIFACT, not an adjective. In-guest KATs from the NIST/reference vectors for all three schemes, the exact reference implementation and commit vendored, and a NEGATIVE test proving a tampered signature is rejected inside the guest. Cite the OpenZeppelin ML-DSA stub (returns true for any 43-felt key and 79-felt signature) as the reason this is mandatory. Write "FIPS 204 / FIPS 205 conformant" for ML-DSA and SLH-DSA, and "Falcon spec, FIPS 206 draft" for Falcon-512, never "FIPS-conformant Falcon".

10. PRE-REGISTER THE MAX N and report where each prover OOMs. If the sweep only reaches small N, present a table of measured points, not a fitted curve, and state the ~3 GB RISC Zero guest memory ceiling and the 64 GB host as the binding constraints.

11. FRAME AMORTIZATION AS REPLICATION, citing ePrint 2021/1048 Table 3, the Winterfell README, ZK-ACE Table 7 and leanBench in the same breath. The novel part is the FIPS schemes and the precompile-asymmetry decomposition, not the shape of the curve.
counterevidence:
Several things cut against the severity of my critique, and I should state them.

1. The empty cell is real and I could not refute it. Everything I checked confirms no published prover-time/RAM figures for ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512 or SLH-DSA-128s inside a general-purpose zkVM. A reviewer who dismisses this work has nothing better to cite, which is a strong position for the authors.

2. The de facto standard in this subfield is much lower than the one I am applying. ZK-ACE (arXiv 2603.07974, v3) published Apple Silicon Criterion medians with the authors' own admission that no reproducible artifact accompanies them, and it is on its third revision. leanBench publishes GCP numbers and openly discloses "No turbo / governor pinning baked in". HAPPIER (LightSec 2025, Springer) published EXTRAPOLATED timings from runs that aggregated only two real signatures, with no memory and no hardware named, and got into LNCS. Measured wall-clock on named hardware with runnable code would already be above all three. My objections describe the bar for a crypto venue, not the bar this literature actually enforces.

3. Single-platform benchmarks are routinely published and cited when the caveats travel with the numbers. ZK-ACE explicitly lists "Single-platform benchmarks" as a stated limitation and survives. So a laptop benchmark is not automatically disqualifying; an UNDISCLOSED laptop benchmark is.

4. On O1 specifically, I am making an inference the vendors do not state: that the unique decoding regime costs more queries per security bit than the conjectured regimes. That is standard coding-theory reasoning, but neither docs page says it, and I did not measure the prover-cost consequence. The authors could legitimately push back that the practical delta is small relative to the PQ-vs-classical gap they are measuring. My response would be that this is exactly why the parameters must be published, so a reader can judge, but I should not assert the magnitude.

5. I could not read ePrint 2026/858 (HTTP 403, Cloudflare), so my characterisation of the late-2025 disproof of the up-to-capacity proximity gap conjecture rests on a search snippet, not a fetched primary source. I have deliberately kept that out of my objections and relied only on the two vendor security pages I fetched directly. Anyone citing that development must obtain the PDF via Wayback or a browser first.

6. On the $/tx objection: if the benchmark's audience is protocol designers choosing a settlement scheme, a transparently derived cost model is genuinely useful even without a market rate. My objection is to it being a HEADLINE metric, not to its existence.

lens: Wording / overclaim risk: interrogating each load-bearing word in the positioning sentence for defensibility against the verified prior-art corpus.
reasoning:
The SUBSTANCE survives. The SENTENCE does not. Attacking it word by word:

**1. "SLH-DSA-128s" IS NOT A REAL PARAMETER SET NAME. (fatal at copy level, trivial to fix)**
I extracted the parameter-set names directly from the FIPS 205 PDF (https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/FIPS/NIST.FIPS.205.pdf, published 2024-08-13, accessed 2026-07-23). The full set found in the document text: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s, SLH-DSA-SHA2-128f, SLH-DSA-SHA2-192s, SLH-DSA-SHA2-192f, SLH-DSA-SHA2-256s, SLH-DSA-SHA2-256f, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128f, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-192s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-192f, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-256s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-256f. A grep for the bare string "128s" preceded by "SLH-DSA-" with no hash family returned nothing. **There is no "SLH-DSA-128s".** In a claim whose entire differentiator is the word "standards-conformant", naming a parameter set that the standard does not define is self-refuting on sight. It also hides a real measurement decision: SHA2-128s and SHAKE-128s are different workloads with different precompile stories (SP1 has a sha3/SHAKE precompile; RISC Zero has a patched `tiny-keccak`; both have sha2 patches), so the choice materially moves the number. Note the sloppy form is in circulation — a web search summary handed it back to me verbatim as "SLH-DSA-128s ... signatures of 7,856 bytes" — which is exactly why using it marks you as downstream of blog posts rather than the standard.

**2. "Falcon-512" IS NOT A FIPS SCHEME. The framing "the three FIPS schemes specifically" is FALSE.**
FIPS 206 (FN-DSA) is not published. Per NIST-adjacent reporting checked 2026-07-23, "NIST submitted the draft standard for FN-DSA (FIPS 206) for approval on August 28, 2025" and it is still expected as an Initial Public Draft with "the final standard expected in late 2026 or early 2027" (https://www.digicert.com/blog/quantum-ready-fndsa-nears-draft-approval-from-nist; corroborated by encryptionconsulting.com and NIST's own FIPS 206 status presentation, csrc.nist.gov). The corpus already contains an independent confirmation from a source with no incentive to hedge: OpenZeppelin's cairo-pq-verifiers `schemes.json` labels every Falcon row "draft (FIPS 206)". So "standards-conformant" cannot mean the same thing for all three schemes: FIPS 204 (final) for ML-DSA-44, FIPS 205 (final) for SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s, and **Falcon spec v1.2 / falcon.py-interoperable, FIPS 206 not final** for Falcon-512. Never write "the three FIPS schemes". Never write "FIPS-conformant Falcon".

**3. "first" — drop it. It is unverifiable in principle and brittle in practice.**
(a) You cannot claim a negative over a literature you demonstrably cannot read. HAPPIER (LightSec 2025 / LNCS 16216) is paywalled — Unpaywall returns `{"is_oa": false, "oa_status": "closed"}` — and its full text was never read. You do not know whether its tables contain a memory column. Asserting "first" over a corpus containing at least one unread paywalled paper in exactly your area is an unforced error.
(b) The qualifier stack is doing too much work. HAPPIER already published prover-time-vs-batch-size (N=128…262144, merge arity 2/4/8) for a post-quantum hash-based signature aggregated **inside RISC Zero**. Your claim survives only because that scheme is generalized-XMSS-over-SHA rather than a standardized FIPS scheme. That is a real distinction, but it means "first" is load-bearing on two adjectives, and a reviewer skimming will reasonably read your sentence as "first PQ signature prover cost in a zkVM" — which is dead, twice over (ePrint 2021/1048 in 2022 for a bespoke AIR, HAPPIER in RISC Zero).
(c) Seven-conjunct firsts read as narrowing-to-fit. Counting: first + measured + standards-conformant + three named schemes + general-purpose zkVM + two named provers + same-rig classical control + batch sweep. Each is individually true and collectively it looks like a claim engineered backwards from a result.
(d) "First" ages badly against live repos. starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow has `- [ ] Stwo proving benchmarks` unchecked as of 2026-07-23, and OpenZeppelin's ml_dsa_44 crate is an explicit stub. Either could land numbers on any commit. An existence claim about the literature is stable; a priority claim is one push away from being wrong.
**Replacement that is strictly stronger because it is falsifiable and shows work:** "We could not find a published measurement of X. LITERATURE.md lists every source we checked and how each differs." That invites correction instead of daring refutation, and it converts your best asset — the verified prior-art corpus — into visible credibility.

**4. "public" — near-vacuous, delete.** Every competitor is public too (BTQ blog, s2morrow, leanBench, OpenZeppelin, HAPPIER's repo). It differentiates nothing. If you want the real property, say "openly licensed, raw samples committed, one command to reproduce".

**5. "reproducible" — keep it, but it must be scoped or it is your biggest liability.** This is the word people will actually test, and the corpus shows exactly how projects fail it: Dilithium-ZK's linked repo is empty (0 bytes, no commits) and its manifest excludes `tests/`, `examples/`, `scripts/`; s2morrow's `make *-prove` targets depend on `ssh://git@github.com/m-kus/proving-utils.git`, which does not resolve publicly; HAPPIER's headline times are a closed-form extrapolation from runs that aggregate two real signatures; ZK-ACE's own §12.2 concedes no reproducible artifact accompanies Table 7. You will be graded against those failures. But your own environment has holes you must disclose in the same breath, or "reproducible" becomes the thing you get caught on: RISC Zero's Groth16 prover is documented as x86-only and "Apple Silicon is currently unsupported (even via Docker)"; SP1 has no GPU path on macOS (CUDA is Linux x86_64 only, AVX is Intel-only) while RISC Zero uses Metal on the same machine; SP1's default `segment`/toolchain and RISC Zero's `segment_limit_po2` default are unpublished and set peak RAM. Say "reproducible on the named machine with pinned toolchain versions", and publish a NOT-REPRODUCIBLE-HERE list.

**6. "cross-prover" / "same rig" implies a comparability that does not exist.** SP1 v6.x guests target `riscv64im-succinct-zkvm-elf` (RV64IM); RISC Zero targets `riscv32im-risc0-zkvm-elf`. An SP1 cycle and a RISC Zero cycle are different ISAs. On the M3 Max, RISC Zero gets Metal acceleration and SP1 gets scalar CPU. And the precompile field is unequal in BOTH directions: both accelerate Ed25519/ECDSA, neither has an NTT/lattice precompile, but RISC Zero DOES have a patched `tiny-keccak` (Keccak-f[1600] → SHAKE) with the keccak feature stabilized in v3.0.1, so "RISC Zero has no SHAKE acceleration" would be wrong. "Measured on the same rig" is true and fine; "comparable across provers" is not, unless the disclosure travels with every chart.

**7. "matrix" (from the lens; NOT in the current claim — keep it that way).** A matrix promises full cells. You will have holes: no Groth16 on aarch64, possibly no SLH-DSA-SHAKE-256s at feasible batch sizes, non-uniform acceleration per cell. Say "sweep" or "grid of measured configurations", never "matrix" or "complete".

**8. "cross-scheme" (from the lens; also not in the claim).** Harmless but empty — you already name the three schemes, which is more informative. Don't add it.

**9. "prover cost" invites $/proof, which you cannot source.** Bonsai was shut down ("As of December 2025, Bonsai is no longer available", docs.boundless.network) and Boundless is a bid-based market with no published rate. Always expand: "prover cost (wall-clock time, peak RSS, proof bytes)".

**10. "swept across batch size" must be framed as a control, not a finding.** Flat amortization is settled — ePrint 2021/1048's Table 3, the Winterfell README's explicit "Trace time and prover RAM ... grow pretty much linearly", ZK-ACE Table 7 (prove/tx 14.29→15.76 ms flat while proof/tx falls 122→13 KB), and leanBench's committed JSON (my derivation: 10.65/9.83/9.74/9.92 ms per sig at N=125/250/500/1000). If the sweep reads as the headline discovery you get embarrassed by three separate citations.

**11. Pre-empt the substitution accusation.** Readers primed by s2morrow's Poseidon hash-to-point and OpenZeppelin's BLAKE2s variants will assume you cheated. State affirmatively: standard SHAKE/SHA2 per the spec, accelerated implementations permitted (RISC Zero's patched tiny-keccak, SP1's sha3 precompile) but never substituted primitives — and, ideally, publish both the accelerated and stock-crate numbers, which is itself an unmeasured result.

**12. Voice/legal nits.** "We publish" from an unaffiliated project reads as institutional; say so explicitly ("independent, unaffiliated with RISC Zero, Succinct, or StarkWare"). Apache-2.0 §6 grants no trademark rights — describe, don't brand.

**Verdict:** the position is real and the empty cell is real (OpenZeppelin, a top-tier auditor, ships an ML-DSA-44 crate whose `verify` is a length check; zk-X509 (arXiv 2603.25190, 26 Mar 2026) measures ECDSA P-256 at "11.8M cycles" and RSA-2048 at 17.4M **on SP1** and touches no PQ scheme at all). But the sentence as written contains a parameter set that does not exist, calls Falcon a FIPS scheme, and stakes everything on an unverifiable "first". Fix the nouns, delete the priority claim, and the claim becomes both true and harder to attack.
counterevidence:
- FIPS 205 (https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/FIPS/NIST.FIPS.205.pdf, 2024-08-13, text extracted 2026-07-23): the only SLH-DSA names present are SLH-DSA-{SHA2,SHAKE}-{128,192,256}{s,f}. "SLH-DSA-128s" does not occur.
- FIPS 206 / FN-DSA is NOT final: "NIST submitted the draft standard for FN-DSA (FIPS 206) for approval on August 28, 2025", final "expected in late 2026 or early 2027" (https://www.digicert.com/blog/quantum-ready-fndsa-nears-draft-approval-from-nist, accessed 2026-07-23). Corroborated in-corpus by OpenZeppelin cairo-pq-verifiers `schemes.json`: "draft (FIPS 206)".
- HAPPIER (LightSec 2025, LNCS 16216) already published prover-time-vs-batch-size for a PQ hash-based signature **in RISC Zero** — and is paywalled (`oa_status: closed`), so its full contents are unknown to us. Both facts cut against "first".
- zk-X509, arXiv 2603.25190, v1 26 Mar 2026, accessed 2026-07-23: "Evaluated on the SP1 zkVM, the system achieves 11.8M cycles for ECDSA P-256 (17.4M for RSA-2048)". Classical only, no PQ scheme — supports the empty cell, and shows the field reports CYCLES, not wall-clock, which is a differentiator worth naming instead of "first".
- Flat amortization is triply established (ePrint 2021/1048 Table 3; ZK-ACE Table 7; leanBench committed JSON), so the batch sweep is a control, not a discovery.
- ISA mismatch: SP1 v6.x guest target `riscv64im-succinct-zkvm-elf` vs RISC Zero `riscv32im-risc0-zkvm-elf` — cross-prover cycle comparison is invalid without disclosure.
- RISC Zero DOES ship a patched `tiny-keccak` (Keccak-f[1600]/SHAKE), keccak feature "stabilized" in v3.0.1 — so any copy asserting "no SHAKE acceleration in RISC Zero" is wrong.
- No citable $/proof: "As of December 2025, Bonsai is no longer available" (docs.boundless.network); Boundless pricing is bid-based.
recommended_change:
**A. Rewritten claim (strongest fully defensible version — replaces the sentence under attack):**

"PQ-STARK-BENCH measures what it costs to *prove* post-quantum signature verification inside a general-purpose zkVM. We publish wall-clock prover time, peak resident memory and proof size for **ML-DSA-44 (FIPS 204)**, **SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s (FIPS 205)** and **Falcon-512 (Falcon spec v1.2; FN-DSA / FIPS 206 is still draft)**, verified with their specified hash functions — accelerated implementations, never substituted primitives, so no Poseidon or BLAKE2s hash-to-point — inside **RISC Zero** and **SP1**, alongside **Ed25519 and secp256k1 ECDSA controls run through the same harness on the same machine**, swept across batch size. Every figure is on named hardware with pinned toolchain versions, raw per-iteration samples committed, and a single command to reproduce. Precompile support is unequal across schemes and provers and the two zkVMs target different RISC-V ISAs; both are disclosed on every chart. Batch sweeps confirm what prior work already showed — prover time and memory grow with N, only proof bytes amortize — and exist here as a control, not a finding. **We could not find a prior publication of these figures; LITERATURE.md lists every source we checked and exactly how each differs.** Independent project, unaffiliated with RISC Zero, Succinct or StarkWare."

**B. Site-hero variant (short):**

> ### How much does it cost to *prove* a post-quantum signature?
> Measured prover time, peak memory and proof size for **ML-DSA-44**, **Falcon-512** and **SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s** inside **RISC Zero** and **SP1** — with a classical Ed25519 / ECDSA control on the same machine, across batch sizes.
> Named hardware. Pinned versions. Raw samples committed. One command to reproduce.

(Ultra-short strapline if needed: *"Measured prover cost for post-quantum signatures inside RISC Zero and SP1. Named hardware, reproducible."*)

**C. Phrases that must NEVER appear on the site:**

*Naming / standards errors*
1. "SLH-DSA-128s" (and any SLH-DSA name without SHA2 or SHAKE) — not a FIPS 205 parameter set
2. "the three FIPS schemes" / "all three FIPS signature standards" — Falcon is not one
3. "FIPS-conformant Falcon" / "FIPS 206 Falcon" / "the FIPS Falcon standard" — FIPS 206 is not final
4. "SPHINCS+" used interchangeably with SLH-DSA in a results table (name the exact parameter set)
5. "Dilithium" as the measured scheme name (say ML-DSA-44)

*Priority claims*
6. "the first" / "world's first" / "first-ever" — in any construction
7. "nobody has measured this" / "no one has ever benchmarked" / "unprecedented"
8. "the first PQ signature verified in a STARK" — refuted by ePrint 2021/1048 (2022)
9. "the first PQ signature prover cost in a zkVM" — refuted by HAPPIER (RISC Zero, LightSec 2025)
10. "novel" / "breakthrough" / "we discovered"

*Amortization*
11. "we show how prover cost amortizes with batch size" framed as a finding
12. "batching makes PQ signatures cheap/practical" — prover time and RAM do not amortize
13. any headline implying per-signature prover cost falls with N

*Comparability*
14. "apples-to-apples across provers" / "head-to-head" without the ISA + acceleration disclosure
15. "identical conditions" / "level playing field" — precompile support is unequal by construction
16. "RISC Zero has no SHAKE acceleration" — false (patched tiny-keccak, stabilized v3.0.1)
17. "PQ is Nx slower than classical" as a bare number — that ratio is mostly precompile asymmetry
18. any chart putting Cairo steps, L2 gas, or zkVM cycles on the same axis as prover seconds

*Reproducibility / completeness*
19. "complete matrix" / "the full matrix" / "every combination" — there will be empty cells
20. "runs anywhere" / "works on any machine" — Groth16 is x86-only on RISC Zero; SP1 has no GPU on macOS
21. "$X per proof" from any vendor price — Bonsai is dead, Boundless is bid-based
22. "audited" / "production-ready" / "secure" — nothing here is audited

*Voice*
23. "we prove PQ settlement is ready/viable" — you measured cost, not readiness
24. "official" / any RISC Zero, Succinct, StarkWare, or NIST branding implying endorsement
25. "settlement is already post-quantum" — Pedersen, secp syscalls and KZG blob DA are not
